response:
SECTION 1  
There are two tree hollows in a cedar. A squirrel sitting in front of the second hollow said:  
1) There are no nuts in the other hollow,  
2) There is at least one nut in one of the hollows.  

SECTION 2  
The statements suggest that the squirrel is making a claim about the presence of nuts in both hollows. If the squirrel were a red squirrel (truth-teller), then both statements would have to be true. However, if the squirrel were a gray squirrel (liar), then both statements would have to be false.  

SECTION 3  
If the squirrel were a red squirrel, then both statements would be true. That would mean:  
1) There are no nuts in the other hollow (so the first hollow has no nuts),  
2) There is at least one nut in one of the hollows (which is true if the second hollow has nuts).  

SECTION 4  
But if the squirrel were a gray squirrel, then both statements would be false. That would mean:  
1) There are nuts in the other hollow (so the first hollow has nuts),  
2) There are no nuts in one of the hollows (which is true if the second hollow has no nuts).  

SECTION 5  
This contradiction shows that the squirrel cannot be a red squirrel, because it would make a true statement about the presence of nuts in the other hollow. Therefore, the squirrel must be a gray squirrel.  

SECTION 6  
The squirrel is a gray squirrel.
